quiz.q:
Speed at the bottom of a drop

Problem. A pallet slips from a rack and falls 2.5 m. Find: its impact speed, and show the mass does not matter.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Conservation, drop from rest: \( mgh = \tfrac12 mv^2 \). (2) Mass cancels: \( v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.5} = 7.0\ \mathrm{m/s} \).

Answer: ≈7 m/s (25 km/h), any mass. Sanity check: mass cancels in the SPEED — but not in the energy: a 50 kg pallet arrives with 1.2 kJ, a 500 kg one with 12 kJ, at the same 7 m/s. Same velocity, tenfold consequence — the distinction the momentum lesson formalizes next.

quiz.q:
The drop hammer's exchange

Problem. A 5 kg hammer head is raised 1.2 m and released to swing to its lowest point. Find: its speed and kinetic energy at the bottom, ignoring pivot losses.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.2} = 4.85\ \mathrm{m/s} \). (2) \( KE = \tfrac12 \times 5 \times 4.85^2 = 58.9\ \mathrm{J} \) — exactly the \( mgh = 5 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 \) invested.

Answer: ≈4.9 m/s carrying ≈59 J. Sanity check: the path — arc, ramp, or free drop — never entered the calculation; conservative forces pay by endpoints only. That path-independence is why energy methods dispatch swinging and sliding problems that force methods turn into geometry exercises.

quiz.q:
What friction collected on the ramp

Problem. A 20 kg crate leaves the top of a 3.0 m-high ramp from rest and arrives at the bottom at 5.0 m/s. Find: the energy lost to friction and the fraction of the invested energy it represents.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Invested: \( mgh = 20 \times 9.81 \times 3.0 = 589\ \mathrm{J} \). (2) Delivered: \( \tfrac12 mv^2 = 0.5 \times 20 \times 25 = 250\ \mathrm{J} \). (3) Lost: \( 589 - 250 = 339\ \mathrm{J} \) — 58% of the investment, now heat in the ramp surface.

Answer: ≈339 J (58%) to friction. Sanity check: the frictionless arrival would have been \( \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3} = 7.7\ \mathrm{m/s} \); the observed 5.0 m/s < 7.7 respects the bound, and the shortfall MEASURED the friction without a single coefficient — the ledger used as an instrument.

quiz.q: Choosing where \( h = 0 \) sits for gravitational potential energy:
choices: Is free — only PE differences enter the physics
Must be the lowest point in the problem
quiz.solution: PE is defined up to a constant: shift the reference and every state's PE shifts equally, leaving all DIFFERENCES — and therefore all speeds and forces — untouched. Any convenient level works, provided it is declared once and never moved mid-problem; moving it mid-ledger is the bookkeeping error that creates energy from nothing. Convention favors the lowest point purely for tidy positive numbers.

quiz.q: Mechanical energy \( (KE + PE) \) is conserved when:
choices: Only conservative forces (gravity, springs) do work
quiz.solution: Conservation is a property of WHICH forces do work, not of speed or path shape: gravity and springs repay in full; friction and drag divert energy to heat and break the mechanical ledger (though never the universal one). 'No forces' is unnecessarily strong — forces that do zero work, like normals, are harmless. The gatekeeper question of the working method is exactly this classification.

quiz.q: A spring of stiffness \( k = 800\ \mathrm{N/m} \) is compressed 50 mm. The stored energy is:
quiz.solution: \( PE = \tfrac12 kx^2 = 0.5 \times 800 \times (0.05)^2 = 1.0\ \mathrm{J} \) — with x in METRES; feeding millimetres unconverted inflates the answer by \( 10^6 \). Choosing 40 forgets the square (\( \tfrac12 kx \) is a force-flavored hybrid, 20 N, not an energy); 20 forgets the half. Small spring energies still snap fingers: 1 J released in milliseconds is a hundred-watt event.

quiz.q: A crate slides to rest on a level floor. Its kinetic energy has:
choices: Become heat at the sliding interface
quiz.solution: Friction's negative work routes the energy to thermal accounts — the interface warms by exactly the kinetic energy spent. Nothing is destroyed (nature forbids it), no height changed (no PE), and energy does not flow backward up a drivetrain uninvited. The heat is usually too diffuse to notice — but brakes, clutches, and dragging belts make the same accounting vivid and occasionally expensive.

quiz.q: An object dropped from height \( h \) passes the half-height point at what fraction of its final speed?
quiz.solution: Speed goes as the square root of height fallen: at \( h/2 \), \( v = \sqrt{2g(h/2)} = \sqrt{gh} \), against a final \( \sqrt{2gh} \) — ratio \( 1/\sqrt{2} \). Half the DROP buys 71% of the speed (and half the energy). The intuition that speed accrues linearly with height is the error; the square root front-loads the gain, exactly as the braking-distance square did in reverse.

8.lecture:
§1 · Momentum: motion's currency

Momentum, \( \mathbf{p} = m\mathbf{v} \), is the quantity of motion — a vector, in kilogram-metres per second. Newton's second law in its original and most general form says force is the RATE of change of momentum: \( \mathbf{F} = d\mathbf{p}/dt \). Integrated over a contact, this becomes the impulse–momentum theorem: \( \mathbf{F}\Delta t = \Delta\mathbf{p} \) — the product of force and its duration equals the momentum change it produces. The product is the point: the same momentum change can be bought with a large force briefly or a small force at leisure, and nearly all impact engineering — packaging, bumpers, crash padding, soft-landing programs — is the deliberate purchase of more \( \Delta t \) to shrink \( F \).

§2 · Conservation: the system keeps its total

When no net EXTERNAL force acts on a system, its total momentum cannot change — the internal pushes and pulls cancel in third-law pairs. Collisions, couplings, and separations all obey it: \( m_1v_1 + m_2v_2 \) before equals the same sum after, signs carried by direction. The law holds regardless of how violent, brief, or lossy the interaction is — which is what makes it the collision analyst's first equation, written before anything is known about the forces themselves.

§3 · Collisions: what momentum keeps, energy may lose

Momentum is conserved in every collision; kinetic energy is not. A perfectly INELASTIC collision — the bodies couple and move together — conserves momentum while converting the maximum possible kinetic energy to deformation and heat. An ELASTIC collision conserves both, an idealization that hard steel balls and billiards approach. Real impacts live between the limits. The two conservation questions are therefore always asked separately, and the energy shortfall in a sticking collision is not an error — it is the crumple, the dent, and the noise, priced exactly.

§4 · The impulse–momentum working method

Define the system so the interesting forces become internal — both carts, hammer plus pile — and check that external forces are absent, negligible, or perpendicular during the brief event. Declare the positive direction and carry every velocity's sign. Write momentum conservation across the event; solve; then, if energies matter, audit the kinetic-energy books separately and route any loss honestly to deformation and heat. For contact-force questions, use the theorem in its \( F = \Delta p/\Delta t \) form and respect its sensitivity: halving the stopping time doubles the average force, which is why hard surfaces break what padded ones spare. The method's discipline is the system boundary — drawn well, the messy contact forces never need computing at all; drawn badly, nothing conserves and everything confuses.

Momentum and energy together complete the collision toolkit: momentum answers the velocities, energy names the damage, and the pair of audits — run separately, never merged — resolves every impact question this course will pose. The dynamics course will add rotation to both ledgers; the bookkeeping discipline installed here is the part that does not change.
8.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Second and third laws (Lesson 4) — conservation is their joint consequence.
  • Vectors and signs (Lesson 1) — momentum is direction-carrying.
  • Kinetic energy (Lesson 6) — the second, separate audit in collisions.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Momentum\( \mathbf{p} = m\mathbf{v} \)The motion currency; vector, kg·m/s.
Impulse–momentum theorem\( F\,\Delta t = \Delta p \)Trading force against time at fixed momentum change.
Conservation\( \Sigma p \) constant, no net external forceCollisions, couplings, separations — the first equation written.
Perfectly inelasticbodies couple; max KE lossCoupling wagons, catching parts, sticking impacts.
Elasticmomentum AND KE conservedThe hard-ball idealization; real impacts sit between.
Impact force estimate\( F = \Delta p/\Delta t \)Why stopping time, not speed alone, decides damage.
System boundarychoose so contact forces are internalThe move that makes the messy forces irrelevant.

quiz.q:
The price of a hard stop

Problem. A 0.5 kg part arrives at 4 m/s and must be stopped. Find: the average force if it stops in 0.02 s (rigid stop) versus 0.2 s (cushioned stop).
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Momentum to remove: \( \Delta p = 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0\ \mathrm{kg\,m/s} \). (2) Rigid: \( F = 2.0/0.02 = 100\ \mathrm{N} \). (3) Cushioned: \( F = 2.0/0.2 = 10\ \mathrm{N} \).

Answer: 100 N versus 10 N — tenfold softer for tenfold longer. Sanity check: the product \( F\Delta t \) is identical (2.0 N·s) in both cases — the momentum bill must be paid in full; only the installment plan changes. Every bumper, dashpot, and packaging foam is this calculation cast in hardware.

quiz.q:
Coupling on the line

Problem. A 5 kg carrier moving at 4 m/s couples into an identical stationary carrier. Find: the common speed after coupling, and the kinetic energy lost.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Momentum: \( 5 \times 4 + 5 \times 0 = 10\,v \Rightarrow v = 2.0\ \mathrm{m/s} \). (2) KE before: \( \tfrac12 \times 5 \times 16 = 40\ \mathrm{J} \); after: \( \tfrac12 \times 10 \times 4 = 20\ \mathrm{J} \). (3) Lost: 20 J — exactly half, to the coupling's clunk, flex, and heat.

Answer: 2 m/s; 50% of the kinetic energy gone. Sanity check: equal masses coupling always halve the speed and the kinetic energy — momentum conserved, energy honestly not. The energy did not err; it bought the permanent coupling, and the accounting names its price.

quiz.q:
Recoil of the free trolley

Problem. A 200 kg unbraked trolley carries a 20 kg component. The component is ejected horizontally at 3 m/s (relative to ground). Find: the trolley's recoil velocity.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) System momentum before: zero. (2) After: \( 20 \times 3 + 180 \times v = 0 \Rightarrow v = -60/180 = -0.33\ \mathrm{m/s} \). (3) The 180 kg remainder recoils at 0.33 m/s opposite the ejection.

Answer: ≈0.33 m/s backward. Sanity check: momentum magnitudes match — \( 20 \times 3 = 60 = 180 \times 0.33 \) — and the mass ratio (9:1) appears inverted in the speed ratio, as conservation demands. Every launcher, ejector, and pusher mechanism on an unsecured base rehearses this arithmetic; brakes exist because of it.

quiz.q: Total momentum of a system is conserved when:
choices: No net external force acts on the system
quiz.solution: Conservation follows from Newton's laws whenever external forces sum to zero — internal forces, however violent, cancel in third-law pairs. Elasticity governs the ENERGY question, not the momentum one; rigidity and friction are red herrings at the system level (friction internal to the system is internal). Drawing the boundary so the big forces are internal is the entire craft.

quiz.q: Impulse equals:
choices: The area under the force–time curve
The slope of the momentum curve
quiz.solution: \( J = \int F\,dt \) — force accumulated over its duration, equal to the momentum change it delivers. Force-over-time is a rate, not an accumulation; slope-of-momentum IS force (the differential form); and \( ma \) is instantaneous force, one integral short of impulse. The area reading matters practically: load-cell traces of impacts are integrated to exactly this quantity.

quiz.q: Padded surfaces reduce impact damage because they:
choices: Extend the stopping time, lowering the force for the same momentum change
Reduce the momentum to be removed
quiz.solution: The momentum bill \( \Delta p \) is fixed by mass and speed; padding stretches \( \Delta t \), and \( F = \Delta p/\Delta t \) falls proportionally. Momentum is not absorbed or shrunk — it is transferred at a gentler rate. Crumple zones, dock bumpers, and foam packaging are all \( \Delta t \)-merchants, selling time to buy down force.

quiz.q: In a perfectly inelastic collision, which is conserved?
choices: Momentum only — kinetic energy takes its maximum loss
quiz.solution: Sticking together is the maximum-KE-loss outcome compatible with momentum conservation; the missing energy became deformation, heat, and sound. Both-conserved defines the ELASTIC limit — the other end of the spectrum. Momentum survives every collision without exception; energy's fate depends on the hardware, which is why the two audits are always run separately.

quiz.q: A 1000 kg cart at 2 m/s and a 2000 kg cart at 1 m/s have:
choices: Equal momenta but different kinetic energies
quiz.solution: Momenta: \( 1000\times2 = 2000\times1 = 2000\ \mathrm{kg\,m/s} \) — equal. Energies: \( \tfrac12\times1000\times4 = 2000\ \mathrm{J} \) versus \( \tfrac12\times2000\times1 = 1000\ \mathrm{J} \) — the faster cart carries double. Momentum scales with v, energy with \( v^2 \): two different currencies, and collisions settle accounts in both. The distinction is the entire reason Lessons 6 and 8 are separate lessons.

9.lecture:
§1 · The angular vocabulary

Rotation replays kinematics with angular characters. Angular position \( \theta \) is measured in radians — the natural unit, because it makes arc length \( s = \theta r \) with no conversion factor. Angular velocity \( \omega = d\theta/dt \) in rad/s, and angular acceleration \( \alpha = d\omega/dt \) in rad/s², chain exactly as their linear siblings did, and the constant-\( \alpha \) toolkit is Lesson 2's kit with letters swapped: \( \omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t \), \( \theta = \omega_0 t + \tfrac12\alpha t^2 \), \( \omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha\theta \). The machine floor speaks rpm; physics speaks rad/s; the exchange rate \( \omega = 2\pi N/60 \) is crossed so often it should be reflex — 1450 rpm, the standard 4-pole motor speed, is 152 rad/s.

§2 · The rolling connection

Rotation meets translation at the rim: a point at radius \( r \) moves at \( v = \omega r \) and, under angular acceleration, gains speed at \( a_t = \alpha r \). The relation runs every belt, chain, and traction drive: pulleys sharing a belt share rim speed, so their angular velocities trade inversely with radius — the speed-ratio law of every pulley pair and gear mesh. Radians earn their keep here: the formulas hold ONLY with angles in radians, and feeding degrees or rpm into \( v = \omega r \) is the classic rotational unit error.

§3 · Torque: force's rotational agent

What force is to translation, torque is to rotation: \( \tau = Fr\sin\theta \), the force times its perpendicular lever arm, in newton-metres. The statics course built moments for equilibrium; dynamics now lets them cause angular acceleration. On a belt drive, the torque delivered is the TENSION DIFFERENCE times radius — \( (T_1 - T_2)r \) — because both belt runs pull, in opposition; on a bolted coupling, torque is what the wrench and the torque spec are negotiating. The moment of inertia \( I \), rotation's mass, waits one lesson ahead as torque's partner.

§4 · The rotational working method

Run rotation problems with a currency-exchange discipline. Convert everything to SI angular units at the border — rpm to rad/s, revolutions to radians — work entirely inside, and convert back only for the final answer if the floor wants rpm. Choose the angular toolkit by the same known-versus-wanted matching as Lesson 2, and pass between the angular and linear worlds only through the radius: \( v = \omega r \), \( a_t = \alpha r \), \( s = \theta r \). Audit with the two standard checks — units (a radian is dimensionless, so \( \omega r \) lands in m/s cleanly) and magnitude sense (industrial machines mostly live between 0.5 and 300 rad/s; an answer of 15,000 rad/s is a units error announcing itself). The kinematics is deliberately familiar; fluency in the conversion discipline is what this lesson actually installs, because every machine after it — pumps, gearboxes, turbines — speaks both languages at once.
9.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Linear kinematics (Lesson 2) — the toolkit transfers letter-for-letter.
  • Moments (Statics) — torque as force times perpendicular arm.
  • Radian measure (Math I) — arc = radius × angle, the native unit.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Angular kinematics\( \omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t \), etc.Lesson 2's kit with \( \theta, \omega, \alpha \) — same selection rules.
rpm ↔ rad/s\( \omega = 2\pi N/60 \)The border crossing every machine problem makes; 1450 rpm ≈ 152 rad/s.
Rim speed\( v = \omega r \)Belts, wheels, and cutting speeds; radians only.
Tangential acceleration\( a_t = \alpha r \)How rim speed changes during spin-up and coast-down.
Torque\( \tau = Fr\sin\theta \)Force's turning agent; N·m.
Belt-drive torque\( \tau = (T_1 - T_2)\,r \)The tension DIFFERENCE does the work — both runs pull.
Speed ratio\( \omega_1 r_1 = \omega_2 r_2 \)Shared rim speed across pulleys and meshing gears.

quiz.q:
From nameplate to belt speed

Problem. A motor runs at 1450 rpm carrying a 60 mm-radius pulley. Find: the angular velocity in rad/s and the belt speed.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( \omega = 2\pi N/60 = 2\pi \times 1450/60 = 152\ \mathrm{rad/s} \). (2) \( v = \omega r = 152 \times 0.060 = 9.1\ \mathrm{m/s} \).

Answer: ≈152 rad/s; ≈9.1 m/s of belt. Sanity check: the rim of a 12 cm pulley at motor speed doing ~9 m/s (33 km/h) is entirely typical of belt drives — and the number is why guards exist. The rpm-to-rad/s crossing (×0.1047) is the single most repeated conversion in machine work; owning it cold is the lesson's toll.

quiz.q:
Torque through a belt

Problem. A belt drives a 100 mm-radius pulley with 350 N tension in the tight side and 150 N in the slack side. Find: the torque delivered to the shaft.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Both runs pull the pulley rim in opposite rotational senses; the net turning agent is the difference. (2) \( \tau = (T_1 - T_2)\,r = (350 - 150) \times 0.100 = 20\ \mathrm{N\,m} \).

Answer: 20 N·m. Sanity check: using the tight side alone (35 N·m) forgets that the slack side fights back — the classic belt error; the 200 N difference is also exactly the net force the belt applies to the shaft bearings, which is why belt tension and bearing life are one conversation.

quiz.q:
The fan's spin-up

Problem. A fan accelerates uniformly from rest to 1200 rpm in 8.0 s. Find: the angular acceleration and the number of revolutions completed during the spin-up.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( \omega = 1200 \times 2\pi/60 = 125.7\ \mathrm{rad/s} \); \( \alpha = 125.7/8 = 15.7\ \mathrm{rad/s^2} \). (2) \( \theta = \tfrac12\alpha t^2 = 0.5 \times 15.7 \times 64 = 503\ \mathrm{rad} \). (3) Revolutions: \( 503/2\pi = 80 \).

Answer: ≈15.7 rad/s²; 80 revolutions. Sanity check: average speed during a uniform spin-up is half the final — 600 rpm = 10 rev/s — over 8 s: 80 revolutions ✓. The linear world's triangle-area logic transplants perfectly, which is the entire point of the shared toolkit.

quiz.q: A machine spindle at 3000 rpm is turning at about:
quiz.solution: \( \omega = 2\pi \times 3000/60 = 314\ \mathrm{rad/s} \). Treating rpm as rad/s (3000) skips the conversion; 50 is rev/s mistaken for rad/s (missing the \( 2\pi \)); the huge option multiplies by 60 instead of dividing. The three distractors are precisely the three ways this conversion goes wrong in practice — one per forgotten factor.

quiz.q: Two points on the same rotating disc, one at 50 mm radius and one at 150 mm, have:
choices: The same \( \omega \), but the outer point moves three times faster
quiz.solution: A rigid body shares one angular velocity — every point sweeps the same angle per second — while linear speed grows with radius: \( v = \omega r \), so triple the radius is triple the speed. This is why cutting speed depends on workpiece diameter at fixed spindle rpm, and why rim damage on large rotors is a higher-energy affair than hub damage: same \( \omega \), very different v.

quiz.q: To maximize the torque from a 200 N pull on a 0.3 m wrench, the pull should be applied:
choices: Perpendicular to the wrench, giving 60 N·m
As close to the nut as possible
quiz.solution: \( \tau = Fr\sin\theta \) peaks at \( \sin 90° = 1 \): \( 200 \times 0.3 = 60\ \mathrm{N\,m} \). Pulling along the handle gives zero torque however hard; 45° delivers only 71%; and shrinking r shrinks everything. Statics taught this as moments — rotation now spends the same geometry on causing angular acceleration rather than preventing it.

quiz.q: A shaft turns through 5 complete revolutions. In radians, that is about:
quiz.solution: One revolution is \( 2\pi \) radians: \( 5 \times 2\pi = 31.4\ \mathrm{rad} \). Calling a revolution one radian (5) is the quiet error that breaks every later formula; 1800 converts to degrees, a unit the rotational toolkit never uses. Radians are the native currency because they make \( s = \theta r \) exact — everything else is exchange-rate arithmetic.

quiz.q: A small motor pulley (60 mm) drives a large fan pulley (180 mm) by belt. The fan turns:
choices: At one-third of the motor speed
At three times the motor speed
quiz.solution: The belt imposes one rim speed on both pulleys: \( \omega_1 r_1 = \omega_2 r_2 \), so the triple-radius fan turns at a third the angular speed — and, by the coming lessons, with triple the torque, power flowing through unchanged (minus losses). Every belt-drive speed reduction and every gear ratio is this one equality, applied at the point of contact.

10.lecture:
§1 · Moment of inertia: rotation's mass

Newton's second law rotates into \( \tau = I\alpha \), and the new character \( I = \int r^2\,dm \) — moment of inertia — is mass weighted by the SQUARE of its distance from the axis. The square is everything: the same kilograms placed twice as far resist spin-up four times harder. A solid disc carries \( I = \tfrac12 mr^2 \); a thin hoop, with all mass at the rim, carries \( I = mr^2 \) — double, at equal mass and radius. Distribution, not just quantity, decides rotational reluctance, which is why the statics course's area moment (resisting bending) and this mass moment (resisting angular acceleration) share a name and a square: same mathematics, different physics.

§2 · Rotational energy and the flywheel

A spinning body banks kinetic energy \( KE = \tfrac12 I\omega^2 \), and the \( \omega^2 \) makes speed the cheap lever: doubling spin quadruples storage. That is the flywheel's entire business plan — presses and punches draw their violent, brief demands from a wheel kept spinning by a modest motor, smoothing the load the way the machinery course will later formalize. A body that rolls carries both accounts at once, translational \( \tfrac12 mv^2 \) plus rotational \( \tfrac12 I\omega^2 \), which is why rolling objects arrive at ramp bottoms slower than sliding ones: part of the height budget went into spin.

§3 · Angular momentum and its conservation

Momentum's rotational sibling is \( L = I\omega \), conserved whenever no external torque acts. Because \( I \) depends on mass placement, a system that redistributes its own mass trades \( I \) against \( \omega \) at constant product — the figure skater's spin-up, and equally the reason a machine that throws a mass outward slows its own rotation. Torque is the rate of change of angular momentum, \( \tau = dL/dt \), completing the rotational translation dictionary: every linear law has now crossed over.

§4 · The rotational dynamics working method

Solve rotating problems by the same audit sequence as linear ones, with the dictionary open. Identify the axis and get \( I \) about THAT axis — from the standard shapes, or by the parallel-axis transfer when the spin axis is offset. Sum torques about the axis, signs by a declared positive sense, and apply \( \tau_{net} = I\alpha \). Chain to angular kinematics for speeds and angles, and to \( \tfrac12 I\omega^2 \) when the question is energetic. Audit with limits — at \( \alpha = 0 \) the torque balance must collapse to statics — and with the distribution instinct: between two candidates of equal mass, the one with mass farther out always spins up slower, coasts longer, and stores more at speed. The machines this feeds are immediate: every motor start is \( \tau = I\alpha \) against the driven train's inertia, and every coast-down time is the same equation read backward — which is exactly how the condition-monitoring course will one day read it.
10.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Rotational kinematics (Lesson 9) — \( \omega, \alpha \), and the rpm border.
  • Second law and energy (Lessons 4, 6) — every law here is a translation.
  • Moment of inertia of areas (Statics) — same square, different integrand.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Rotational second law\( \tau = I\alpha \)Net torque about the axis produces angular acceleration.
Moment of inertia\( I = \int r^2\,dm \)Mass times distance SQUARED; distribution decides.
Standard shapesdisc \( \tfrac12 mr^2 \); hoop \( mr^2 \)The two benchmarks bracketing most machine rotors.
Rotational KE\( \tfrac12 I\omega^2 \)Flywheel storage; \( \omega^2 \) makes speed the cheap lever.
Rolling energy\( \tfrac12 mv^2 + \tfrac12 I\omega^2 \)Rolling bodies pay both accounts from one height budget.
Angular momentum\( L = I\omega \); \( \tau = dL/dt \)Conserved without external torque; the I–ω trade.
Coast-down reading\( \alpha = \tau_{friction}/I \)Spin-down time measures drag torque — a field diagnostic.

quiz.q:
Torque to spin up a disc

Problem. A solid 40 kg disc of radius 0.30 m must reach angular acceleration \( 10\ \mathrm{rad/s^2} \). Find: its moment of inertia and the required net torque.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( I = \tfrac12 mr^2 = 0.5 \times 40 \times 0.09 = 1.8\ \mathrm{kg\,m^2} \). (2) \( \tau = I\alpha = 1.8 \times 10 = 18\ \mathrm{N\,m} \).

Answer: \( I = 1.8\ \mathrm{kg\,m^2} \); 18 N·m. Sanity check: were the same 40 kg a rim-concentrated hoop, \( I \) would double to 3.6 and the torque with it — the distribution effect in one comparison. The audit limit holds too: zero α needs zero net torque, statics regained.

quiz.q:
The flywheel's bank balance

Problem. A press flywheel has \( I = 2.5\ \mathrm{kg\,m^2} \) and spins at 150 rad/s. Find: its stored kinetic energy, and the energy released if a punch stroke drops it to 140 rad/s.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( KE = \tfrac12 I\omega^2 = 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 150^2 = 28.1\ \mathrm{kJ} \). (2) At 140 rad/s: \( 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 19600 = 24.5\ \mathrm{kJ} \); released: \( 3.6\ \mathrm{kJ} \).

Answer: ≈28 kJ banked; ≈3.6 kJ delivered per stroke for a 10 rad/s dip. Sanity check: a 6.7% speed dip released 13% of the energy — the \( \omega^2 \) working as designed, letting a small speed sag fund a large punch while the motor tops the account back up between strokes. That is the flywheel's entire economy.

quiz.q:
Conservation in a rebalancing rotor

Problem. A rotating assembly at 20 rad/s has \( I = 1.2\ \mathrm{kg\,m^2} \). An internal mechanism draws its masses inward, reducing \( I \) to \( 0.8\ \mathrm{kg\,m^2} \), with no external torque. Find: the new angular velocity.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) No external torque → \( L = I\omega \) conserved. (2) \( \omega_2 = I_1\omega_1/I_2 = 1.2 \times 20/0.8 = 30\ \mathrm{rad/s} \).

Answer: 30 rad/s — a 50% spin-up from geometry alone. Sanity check: kinetic energy rose from 240 to 360 J — not a violation: the mechanism did work pulling mass inward against centrifugal tendency, and conservation of L (not of KE) governs torque-free rotation. The skater's spin and the governor's droop are this equation in opposite directions.

quiz.q: A solid disc and a thin hoop have equal mass and radius. Under the same torque, which spins up faster?
choices: The disc — its mass sits closer to the axis, so its I is half
quiz.solution: \( I_{disc} = \tfrac12 mr^2 \) versus \( I_{hoop} = mr^2 \): the disc's \( \alpha = \tau/I \) is double. Distribution, not just mass, sets rotational reluctance — the \( r^2 \) weighting means rim mass counts fourfold per doubling of distance. Material is irrelevant at fixed m and geometry. This one comparison is the mental model for every rotor, coupling, and flywheel decision after it.

quiz.q: Doubling a flywheel's speed multiplies its stored energy by:
quiz.solution: \( KE = \tfrac12 I\omega^2 \propto \omega^2 \): double speed, quadruple storage — the same square that governed vehicle kinetic energy, now in rotation. This is why energy-storage flywheels chase speed (and why burst containment becomes their design driver). Eight belongs to \( \omega^3 \), a fan-power law; sixteen to \( \omega^4 \), nothing in this chapter.

quiz.q: Angular momentum of a rotating system is conserved when:
choices: No external torque acts about the axis
quiz.solution: \( \tau_{ext} = dL/dt \): zero external torque freezes L, whatever internal rearrangements occur — that is precisely how the skater and the rebalancing rotor trade I against ω. External FORCES through the axis exert no torque and are harmless; rigidity is not required (the skater isn't rigid). The conservation is the rotational twin of Lesson 8's, with torque replacing force at the gate.

quiz.q: A net torque of 24 N·m acts on a rotor with \( I = 1.5\ \mathrm{kg\,m^2} \). Its angular acceleration is:
quiz.solution: \( \alpha = \tau/I = 24/1.5 = 16\ \mathrm{rad/s^2} \). Multiplying (36) and inverting (0.0625) are the arithmetic traps; forgetting I entirely (24) treats torque as acceleration. The equation is the second law wearing rotational clothes, and the same discipline applies: τ must be the NET torque, friction and load subtracted before dividing.

quiz.q: A cylinder rolls (without slipping) down a ramp while an identical cylinder slides down frictionlessly. At the bottom:
choices: The slider is faster — the roller spent part of its height budget on spin
quiz.solution: Both convert \( mgh \), but the roller must fund two accounts — \( \tfrac12 mv^2 \) AND \( \tfrac12 I\omega^2 \) — so less lands in translation: the slider arrives faster. Mass cancels in both cases. The split depends only on the shape's \( I/mr^2 \) ratio, which is why rolling races between hoops and discs are decided at the starting line by geometry.

11.lecture:
§1 · The restoring recipe

Oscillation needs exactly two ingredients: a restoring force that pulls toward equilibrium in proportion to displacement, and inertia that overshoots it. The spring–mass system is the archetype — \( F = -kx \) feeding \( m\ddot{x} \) — and its motion is simple harmonic: \( x(t) = A\sin(\omega_n t) \), a sine forever, with amplitude \( A \) set by how the motion started and angular frequency set by the system itself: \( \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} \). Stiffness accelerates the cycle; mass slows it; amplitude, remarkably, does not enter — small or large swings keep the same beat, which is what makes natural frequency a property of the MACHINE, not of the disturbance.

§2 · The pendulum and the family of oscillators

The pendulum repeats the recipe with gravity as the spring: \( T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g} \) for small swings — mass absent, length and gravity only. Every oscillator in the family carries the same signature quantities: period \( T = 2\pi/\omega_n \), frequency \( f = 1/T \) in hertz, and the energy sloshing between kinetic (maximum at equilibrium, where speed peaks at \( v_{max} = A\omega_n \)) and potential (maximum at the extremes, where motion pauses). Reading \( x(t) = A\sin(\omega t) \) fluently — amplitude from the coefficient, frequency from the argument — is reading the native language of every vibration trace the later courses will put on screen.

§3 · Why machines care: resonance previewed

Every machine element has natural frequencies, and rotating machinery supplies periodic forcing at running speed and its multiples. When forcing frequency approaches natural frequency, amplitude grows dramatically — resonance — and structures that seemed rigid begin to breathe. This lesson only introduces the actors; the vibrations course will quantify their meeting. But the design instinct starts now: know your \( \omega_n \), know your running speeds, and keep them apart.

§4 · The oscillation working method

Identify the stiffness and the inertia — every oscillator has both, whatever it looks like — and form \( \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} \) (or the pendulum's \( \sqrt{g/L} \)). Convert consciously among \( \omega_n \), \( f \), and \( T \): the \( 2\pi \) that separates rad/s from Hz is this chapter's border crossing, as rpm's was the last one's. Extract amplitude and frequency from any given \( x(t) \) by inspection, and derive the speed and acceleration peaks as \( A\omega \) and \( A\omega^2 \). Audit with the scaling instincts: quadrupling stiffness only doubles frequency (the square root taxes enthusiasm), adding mass always slows the beat, and amplitude never changes the clock. Where a real trace disagrees with these instincts — frequency shifting with load, beat patterns, growth over time — the disagreement is information, and the vibrations course will teach you to read it. Here, the assignment is fluency with the clean case that all the diagnostics measure against — the sine every analyzer decomposes real signals into, and the natural frequency every balancing report quotes. First courses end where careers begin; this one ends on the exact frequency.
11.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Newton's second law (Lesson 4) — SHM is F = ma with a restoring F.
  • Energy conservation (Lesson 7) — the KE–PE slosh at constant total.
  • Radian fluency (Lesson 9) — \( \omega \), f, and T, converted consciously.

Glossary

TermEquationWhen to use
Restoring force\( F = -kx \)Proportional pull toward equilibrium — the oscillation ingredient.
Natural frequency\( \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} \)The machine's own beat; stiffness up, mass down.
Period and frequency\( T = 2\pi/\omega_n,\ f = 1/T \)The 2π border between rad/s and Hz.
Pendulum\( T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g} \)Gravity as the spring; mass cancels, small swings only.
SHM motion\( x = A\sin(\omega_n t) \)Amplitude from the start; frequency from the system.
Peak speed\( v_{max} = A\omega_n \) at equilibriumWhere the energy is all kinetic.
Resonance (preview)forcing ≈ \( \omega_n \) → growthKeep running speeds and natural frequencies apart.

quiz.q:
The isolator's beat

Problem. A 2.0 kg instrument sits on a mount of stiffness 800 N/m. Find: the natural frequency in rad/s and Hz, and the period.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{800/2} = 20\ \mathrm{rad/s} \). (2) \( f = \omega_n/2\pi = 3.18\ \mathrm{Hz} \); \( T = 1/f = 0.314\ \mathrm{s} \).

Answer: 20 rad/s = 3.2 Hz; period ≈0.31 s. Sanity check: quadrupling the stiffness would only double the frequency (√), and doubling the mass would divide it by √2 — the square root damping every intuition of proportionality. A 3 Hz mount under a 24 Hz (1450 rpm) machine is comfortably clear of resonance: the design arithmetic in miniature.

quiz.q:
The inspection pendulum

Problem. A 0.70 m lanyard with a small tool swings as a pendulum. Find: its period, and what changes if the tool is twice as heavy.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) \( T = 2\pi\sqrt{L/g} = 2\pi\sqrt{0.70/9.81} = 1.68\ \mathrm{s} \). (2) Mass does not appear: the heavier tool swings with exactly the same period.

Answer: ≈1.7 s, at any mass. Sanity check: gravity provides both the restoring pull AND acts on the inertia, so mass cancels — the same cancellation as free fall. Only length and gravity remain: to slow a pendulum, lengthen it (fourfold length for double period, the square root again).

quiz.q:
Reading a vibration trace

Problem. A bearing housing's displacement follows \( x(t) = 0.030\sin(12t) \) (metres, seconds). Find: the amplitude, frequency in Hz, and peak velocity.
quiz.solution:
Solution. (1) Amplitude by inspection: \( A = 0.030\ \mathrm{m} = 30\ \mathrm{mm} \). (2) \( \omega = 12\ \mathrm{rad/s} \Rightarrow f = 12/2\pi = 1.9\ \mathrm{Hz} \). (3) \( v_{max} = A\omega = 0.030 \times 12 = 0.36\ \mathrm{m/s} \).

Answer: 30 mm at 1.9 Hz; peak speed 0.36 m/s. Sanity check: peak velocity occurs at mid-swing where the trace crosses zero — displacement and velocity peaks alternate, a quarter-cycle apart. Reading A and ω off a sine by inspection is the exact skill vibration analyzers automate; learning it manually first is what makes their output meaningful.

quiz.q: For small oscillations, increasing a pendulum's swing amplitude:
quiz.solution: Amplitude independence is SHM's defining courtesy: larger swings travel farther but proportionally faster, and the clock holds — which is why pendulums could keep time. (At LARGE angles the approximation frays and the period does grow, but that correction belongs to a later course.) The natural frequency is the system's property; the amplitude merely reports how hard it was disturbed.

quiz.q: To raise a machine mount's natural frequency, you should:
choices: Stiffen the mount or lighten the machine
quiz.solution: \( \omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} \): frequency rises with stiffness and falls with mass. Softening and adding mass LOWER it — which is itself a design tool (isolation mounts deliberately sit far below forcing frequencies). Amplitude has no vote. The k-and-m tuning of natural frequencies against running speeds is the first design instinct of vibration engineering.

quiz.q: In SHM, the speed of the mass is greatest:
quiz.solution: At the extremes the motion pauses (all energy potential); at equilibrium the restoring force has finished accelerating it and the energy is all kinetic: \( v_{max} = A\omega_n \). The energy slosh of Lesson 7 sets this geography — and vibration instruments exploit it, velocity sensors reading peak values a quarter-cycle from displacement peaks.

quiz.q: Quadrupling a spring's stiffness multiplies the oscillation frequency by:
quiz.solution: \( \omega_n \propto \sqrt{k} \): four times the stiffness, twice the frequency — the square root taxing the investment, exactly as it taxed grain refinement and case depth in other courses. Linear thinking (×4) overpays; the lesson's scaling instincts exist to prevent precisely that procurement conversation.

quiz.q: A machine's natural frequency is 24 Hz and its rotor runs at 1440 rpm (24 Hz). The expected behavior is:
choices: Resonant amplitude growth — forcing sits on the natural frequency
quiz.solution: Forcing at the natural frequency is the resonance condition: each cycle's push arrives in step with the motion, and amplitude climbs until damping or damage caps it. The numbers were chosen to collide — 1440 rpm IS 24 Hz. Real design keeps margins between running speeds and natural frequencies precisely to avoid this meeting; the vibrations course quantifies how much margin buys how much peace.
